answers.value:
Isabella is generally healthy and fully able to participate in typical youth activities, including light sports, outdoor games, and online meetings. She does not have any chronic medical conditions that require daily medication, and she has never been hospitalized for asthma, seizures, or other serious conditions. She does not have a peanut allergy or any other known life‑threatening food allergy. 

She occasionally experiences mild seasonal allergies in the spring and early fall (mainly congestion, sneezing, and itchy eyes), likely related to pollen and dust. For this, she sometimes takes an over‑the‑counter antihistamine (such as loratadine) in the mornings if her symptoms are bothering her, but this is not required on a strict schedule. If she ever seems congested or has watery eyes during an activity, this is usually why. She has not had any severe reactions that required an EpiPen or emergency treatment, and no special equipment is needed.

Dietary‑wise, Isabella does not have any medically diagnosed food restrictions, but she and her family try to limit very spicy foods and extremely greasy or fried foods because they can occasionally upset her stomach and make her feel nauseous. She can eat standard snacks like granola bars, crackers, fruit, sandwiches, and most typical event foods (pizza, sandwiches, etc.) without problems. She drinks milk and eats cheese with no issues and does not avoid gluten. She drinks plenty of water and sometimes gets mild headaches if she is outside in the heat too long without enough hydration, so access to water during outdoor activities is helpful.

She wears contact lenses for mild nearsightedness. If her eyes feel dry or irritated (especially with outdoor wind or dust), she may ask to step aside briefly to use rewetting drops or switch to her glasses. This is not a safety concern, but facilitators should know she might need a short break to deal with eye discomfort. She also occasionally gets tension headaches when she has been on a screen for a long time, so if meetings or workshops are extended, allowing brief stretch or screen breaks is helpful.

She does not have any activity limitations for walking, light hiking, team‑building games, or volunteer projects like park clean‑ups. She is comfortable participating in group discussions, role‑plays, and presentations, though she can be a little shy at first. She has no history of fainting, heart problems, or exercise‑induced asthma. If any new medical issues arise during the year (for example, if she is prescribed a new medication or develops a new allergy), her parent, Elena, will notify the coalition and update forms as needed.
